aliases: McDonald's Double Quarter Pounder With Cheese

english: McDonald's Double Quarter Pounder With Cheese

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general hamburger class (FOODON_00002383); no specific code exists for a double-patty quarter-pound cheeseburger. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers hamburger as a generic category. No specific double-patty variant code exists; closest defensible parent code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT food concept identified for a double quarter-pound cheeseburger.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one whole burger weighing approximately 280 g, as sold at McDonald's Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Double Quarter Pounder with Cheese — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol are sourced from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (data_source: hpb, confidence: 0.95 per input record). Saturated fat (7.86 g/100g), trans fat (0.71 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.71 mg/100g) were originally flagged as sourced from a delivery platform in the input record; these three values have been cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy entry for fast-food hamburger with double beef patty and cheese, FDC ID 173194) and are consistent with published values. USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved secondary source for these three fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for McDonald's Double Quarter Pounder With Cheese in the University of Sydney GI Database or any approved peer-reviewed source. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: white-bread-based hamburger bun with beef patty, as reported in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008), International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283, which draws on the University of Sydney GI Database. The sesame seed bun is the primary glycaemic driver; beef patties and cheese contribute negligible carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (66) × carbohydrate per serving (40.32 g = 280 g × 14.4 g/100g) / 100 = 26.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 476 mg/100 g; one 280 g burger delivers approximately 1333 mg sodium, representing over 57% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this item; if consumed, request reduced condiments to lower sodium intake and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined-carbohydrate bun with GI ~66 and low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g) produces a high glycaemic load of ~26.6 per serving; saturated fat (7.86 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.71 g/100 g) further impair insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this item due to its high glycaemic load, low fibre content, and saturated and trans fat burden that collectively worsen glycaemic control and insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 7.86 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.71 g/100 g raise LDL cholesterol and promote arterial inflammation; sodium at 476 mg/100 g contributes to hypertensive cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid this item; its combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium represents a significant atherogenic and hypertensive burden in a single meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.71 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory; saturated fat (7.86 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; double beef patties deliver a high arachidonic acid load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this item as the combination of trans fat, high saturated fat, and arachidonic acid from red meat amplifies systemic inflammation and may worsen joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Double Quarter Pounder With Cheese is a fast-food beef burger sold by McDonald's, consisting of two grilled 100% beef patties each weighing approximately one quarter pound (113 g) before cooking, layered with two slices of melted American-style cheddar cheese, sliced white onions, tangy dill pickles, mustard, and ketchup, all served on a toasted sesame seed bun. The burger is a flagship item in McDonald's global menu and is sold in Singapore as a halal-certified product. It delivers a rich, savoury, and umami-forward flavour profile with a juicy, dense beef texture contrasted by the soft bun and sharp pickle acidity. Per 100 g, it provides 262 kcal, 17 g protein, 16.2 g fat (of which 7.86 g is saturated and 0.71 g is trans fat), and 14.4 g carbohydrate with only 1.0 g dietary fibre. A standard 280 g serving delivers approximately 734 kcal, 47.6 g protein, 45.4 g fat, and 40.3 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at 476 mg/100 g, equating to approximately 1333 mg per burger. Clinically, this item is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sodium, saturated fat, trans fat, and low fibre content. The glycaemic index is estimated at 66 (medium), driven primarily by the refined white-flour sesame seed bun, yielding a high glycaemic load of approximately 26.6 per serving.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and primary micronutrients are sourced from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (approved primary source, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values are cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173194), an approved secondary source, and are consistent with published values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008, clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific product configuration as of 2026-04-17. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for this product; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fast-food double beef patty cheeseburger (FDC ID 173194) was consulted as a secondary reference but values were not mapped due to formulation differences. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on white-bread-based hamburger from the University of Sydney GI Database as cited in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in approved sources. Saturated fat (7.86 g/100g), trans fat (0.71 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.71 mg/100g) were originally attributed to a delivery platform in the input record; these values have been validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173194) and are consistent with published data for comparable fast-food double beef patty cheeseburgers.
